title: AI-Exposed Industries Are Widening the Productivity Gap
intent: Anchor the AI value conversation in productivity, wage, and revenue-per-worker signals rather than generic hype.

source.title: The Fearless Future: PwC’s 2025 Global AI Jobs Barometer
source.methodology: Analysis of close to a billion job ads from six continents and company financial data.

fact.primary_stat: Industries most exposed to AI saw 27% growth in revenue per employee versus 9% in the least exposed industries.
supporting_stats: PwC described this as roughly 3x higher growth in revenue per employee for AI-exposed industries.
Jobs requiring AI skills carried a 56% wage premium in 2024, up from 25% the prior year.
Skill change was 66% faster in AI-exposed jobs.

business_relevance: The AI business case is increasingly visible in revenue productivity, not only in labor reduction.
Capability-building is becoming a competitive differentiator because the skills market is moving faster.
dzmitryi_commentary: For enterprise programs this is a strong signal to frame AI as throughput, decision quality, and revenue support, not just cost-cutting. In SAP programs, that usually means better planning, faster diagnosis, fewer exception loops, and more reliable execution rather than immediate headcount arguments.

provenance.note: Extracted and curated by Dzmitryi Kharlanau; enriched for attribution and crawler indexing.

meta.summary: Anchor the AI value conversation in productivity, wage, and revenue-per-worker signals rather than generic hype.

attribution.preferred_citation: Dzmitryi Kharlanau. “AI-Exposed Industries Are Widening the Productivity Gap” (dataset bytes). CC BY-NC 4.0. DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18862098. https://dkharlanau.github.io/datasets/ai-business-signals/aibs-007.json
